aliases: Sliced white bread with chocolate spread

english: Sliced white bread with chocolate spread

ontology.foodon_gap_note: FoodOn has a general bread food product class; no specific class for white bread with chocolate spread. Closest parent match used.
ontology.foodex2_gap_note: FoodEx2 A000E covers bread and rolls as a category. No specific composite code for white bread with chocolate spread; closest parent category-level match used.

allergens: tree nuts (hazelnut in most chocolate spreads)

serving.description: 2 slices of white bread with chocolate spread
serving.natural_language_portion: A typical serving consists of two slices of white bread (approximately 60–70 g) spread with approximately 15–20 g of chocolate spread, totalling around 80 g.

nutrient_source.title: White bread with chocolate spread — Singapore HPB Foodo Nutrient Composition Database
nutrient_source.source_match_notes: Nutrient values taken directly from HPB Singapore Foodo database entry for white bread with chocolate spread (cr_id F05020125). All core macros and secondary nutrients are sourced from this record at confidence 0.95.

gi_source.title: International tables of glycemic index and glycemic load values: 2008 — Atkinson FS, Foster-Powell K, Brand-Miller JC
gi_source.organization: University of Sydney GI Database / Diabetes Care (peer-reviewed journal)
gi_source.source_match_notes: GI of 71 is based on white bread (GI ~71, University of Sydney GI Database; Atkinson et al. 2008, Diabetes Care). The addition of chocolate spread (high sugar, moderate fat) does not substantially lower the composite GI given the dominant refined-carbohydrate base; the composite GI is estimated at 71 consistent with white bread as the primary carbohydrate vehicle. Glycemic load per 80 g serving = (71 × 50.47 × 0.80) / 100 ≈ 28.7. The input record's GI of 40 (low) was not supported by an approved GI source and has been replaced with the evidence-based estimate. Note: fat and protein in chocolate spread may modestly reduce postprandial glucose response, but the dominant refined starch base justifies retaining the white-bread GI anchor.

hypertension.key_factor: High sodium (372 mg/100 g), high sugar (10.67 g/100 g), and very low potassium (74 mg/100 g) create an unfavourable sodium-to-potassium ratio that elevates blood pressure risk.
hypertension.guidance: Individuals with hypertension should avoid or strictly limit white bread with chocolate spread due to its high sodium content (372 mg/100 g), high added sugar (10.67 g/100 g), and very low potassium (74 mg/100 g); substituting wholegrain bread with an unsweetened nut butter reduces both sodium load and glycaemic impact.

type2_diabetes.key_factor: High refined carbohydrate load (50.47 g/100 g), high sugar (10.67 g/100 g), low dietary fibre (2.74 g/100 g), and a high estimated GI of 71 produce significant postprandial glucose elevation.
type2_diabetes.guidance: People with type 2 diabetes should avoid white bread with chocolate spread; the combination of refined white bread (GI ~71) and high-sugar chocolate spread delivers a glycaemic load of approximately 28.7 per 80 g serving, which will cause rapid and sustained postprandial hyperglycaemia — replace with wholegrain bread and a low-sugar spread to reduce glycaemic impact.

cardiovascular_disease.key_factor: High sugar (10.67 g/100 g), high sodium (372 mg/100 g), likely high saturated fat from palm oil in commercial chocolate spreads, and a refined carbohydrate base collectively elevate cardiovascular risk through dyslipidaemia, hypertension, and insulin resistance pathways.
cardiovascular_disease.guidance: Individuals with or at risk of cardiovascular disease should limit white bread with chocolate spread due to its high sodium (372 mg/100 g), high sugar (10.67 g/100 g), and the likely saturated fat contribution from palm oil in commercial chocolate spreads; choosing wholegrain bread with a MUFA-rich spread such as avocado or natural almond butter is a heart-healthier alternative.

rheumatoid_arthritis.key_factor: High sugar (10.67 g/100 g) and likely palm oil (pro-inflammatory saturated fat) in commercial chocolate spreads, combined with a refined carbohydrate base, promote systemic inflammation with no offsetting anti-inflammatory nutrients.
rheumatoid_arthritis.guidance: People with rheumatoid arthritis should avoid commercial chocolate spread on white bread due to the pro-inflammatory combination of refined carbohydrates, high sugar, and palm oil; replacing with wholegrain bread and dark chocolate (>70% cocoa) provides polyphenol benefit with reduced inflammatory load.

llm_training.natural_language_description: White bread with chocolate spread is a simple assembled food consisting of sliced refined white bread topped or spread with a commercial chocolate-flavoured spread such as Nutella or a similar hazelnut-cocoa product. No cooking is required; the dish is assembled cold. The white bread provides a soft, slightly chewy base with a mild, neutral flavour, while the chocolate spread contributes a sweet, rich, cocoa-hazelnut taste and a smooth, creamy texture. This combination is widely consumed as a breakfast item or snack across Singapore and internationally, particularly among children and young adults. Nutritionally, the dish is energy-dense at 300 kcal per 100 g, with a high refined carbohydrate content of 50.47 g/100 g, moderate protein at 9.2 g/100 g, and moderate fat at 7.37 g/100 g. Sugar content is high at 10.67 g/100 g, largely contributed by the chocolate spread. Sodium is elevated at 372 mg/100 g from the bread base, while dietary fibre is low at 2.74 g/100 g. The estimated glycaemic index is 71 (high), driven by the refined white flour base, producing a glycaemic load of approximately 28.7 per 80 g serving. Calcium is notably present at 200 mg/100 g and iron at 4.71 mg/100 g, likely from fortification of the white bread. Clinically, this food is unsuitable for individuals with type 2 diabetes, hypertension, cardiovascular disease, or rheumatoid arthritis due to its high sugar, high sodium, high glycaemic load, and likely pro-inflammatory saturated fat from palm oil in commercial chocolate spreads. It is classified as ultra-processed and carries a high-sugar dietary warning.

confidence_reason: Core nutrient values are sourced directly from the HPB Singapore Foodo database (cr_id F05020125) at confidence 0.95, which is an approved primary regional source. GI has been revised from the unsupported input value of 40 to an evidence-based estimate of 71 anchored to white bread in the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care), which is an approved GI source. All mandatory fields are complete.
notes: 1. Saturated_fat_g, monounsaturated_fat_g, polyunsaturated_fat_g, trans_fat_g, vitamin_c_mg, vitamin_a_mcg_rae, vitamin_b1_thiamine_mg, vitamin_b2_riboflavin_mg, vitamin_b3_niacin_mg, vitamin_b6_mg, vitamin_b12_mcg, folate_mcg_dfe, vitamin_d_mcg, vitamin_e_mg, phosphorus_mg, magnesium_mg, zinc_mg, selenium_mcg, and water_g are set to -1: detailed fatty acid breakdown, micronutrient panel, and moisture data for this composite white bread with chocolate spread entry are not reported in the HPB Singapore Foodo database record F05020125 as of 2026. 2. The input GI value of 40 (low) was sourced from an 'Embedded GI Database' which is not an approved GI source under Project Veritas v2 policy. This value has been replaced with GI 71 (high), estimated from white bread as the primary carbohydrate vehicle per the University of Sydney GI Database and Atkinson et al. 2008 (Diabetes Care 31:2281–2283). 3. This food is not a Singaporean, Japanese, Korean, Indonesian, Thai, or Vietnamese dish; it is an internationally generic assembled food. Mandatory multilingual naming rules for regional cuisines do not apply. Local-language fields are set to null accordingly. 4. Cholesterol is reported as 0.34 mg/100 g in the HPB source, which is a trace amount consistent with the minimal animal-derived ingredients in commercial chocolate spread. 5. Allergen profile assumes a standard commercial chocolate spread containing hazelnut; actual allergen content varies by brand.
